coredata.dc:title: Fabrication and characterization of novel cRGD modified graphene quantum dots for chemo-photothermal combination therapy
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Chen
Chen, Yu
Xu, Zhizhong
Chen, Binbin
Zhang, Yan
Yi, Xue
Li, Jinming
coredata.dc:description: In this work, an innovative drug delivery system based on cyclic RGD-modified (as the targeting agent) and doxorubicin-loaded graphene quantum dots (R–GQDs@DOX) was fabricated for chemo-photothermal combination therapy. These tasks include drug loading capacity, photothermal efficiency, cellular uptake and combination therapeutic effect of R–GQDs@DOX. The structure of R–GQDs@DOX was characterized by FT-IR spectrum, UV–vis spectrum and AFM. The results indicated that the doxorubicin (DOX) could be loaded onto graphene quantum dots (GQDs) via van der Waals interaction (π–π stacking) and the drug loading capacity was 96.6 % (wt). The R–GQDs@DOX not only showed obvious pH-responsive release of DOX, but also exhibited great photothermal efficacy for cancer cells. The fluorescence intensity indicated that R–GQDs@DOX was effectively taken up by SK–mel–5 and H460 cells. The combination of chemotherapy and photothermal therapy could more effectively inhibit growth of tumor cells, compared to photothermal therapy or chemotherapy alone.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Composite drug-loaded carrier
Synergetic therapy
Drug delivery
Cellular imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Construction of multifunctional graphene quantum dots
Synthesis of cRGD–modified graphene quantum dots (R–GQDs)
Loading R–GQDs with DOX (R–GQDs@DOX) and its characterization
Physical and chemical properties of R–GQDs@DOX
Fabrication and characterization of the R–GQDs@DOX
Physical and chemical properties of R–GQDs@DOX
In vitro chemotherapy and photothermal therapy

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration outlining the fabrication of R–GQDs@DOX nanoparticles.

A: FT-IR spectra of (Ⅰ) GQDs, (Ⅱ) PEG modified GQDs, and (Ⅲ) cRGD modified GQDs–PEG. B: UV analysis of DOX, GQDs, R–GQDs and R–GQDs@DOX measured by UV–vis spectrophotometer; Inset: optical imaging of R-GQDs under daylight (right) and 365 nm UV lamp (left). C: Fluorescence spectra of DOX, R–GQDs, GQDs, GQDs@DOX and R–GQDs@DOX measured at 485 nm.
AFM observation of GQDs@DOX and R–GQDs@DOX.
Zeta potential and size distribution of GQDs@DOX and R–GQDs@DOX. Top: zeta potential; bottom: size distribution.

A: Infrared thermal images of the R–GQDs (100 μg/mL) under NIR irradiation (808 nm, 2 W/cm2). B: Temperature elevation of R–GQDs with different concentrations evaluated under NIR irradiation (808 nm, 2 W/cm2).
Intracellular uptake and localization of DOX (100 μg/mL), GQDs, GQDs@DOX (100 μg/mL for DOX) and R–GQDs@DOX (100 μg/mL for DOX) in H460 cells with incubation for 2 h (scale bar: 50 μm).
Intracellular uptake and localization of DOX (100 μg/mL), GQDs, GQDs@DOX (100 μg/mL for DOX) and R–GQDs@DOX (100 μg/mL for DOX) in SK–mel–5 cells with incubation for 1 h (scale bar: 50 μm).

(AB): Relative cell viability of different cell lines after treatment with DOX, R–GQDs and R–GQDs@DOX for 24 h without and with 5 min NIR irradiation. # P < 0.05, compared with R–GQDs, DOX and R–GQDs@DOX group (t-test). C: The fluorescence intensity of R–GQDs and R–GQDs@DOX evaluated in different cell lines, * P < 0.05, compared with GQDs@DOX (t-test). D: in vitro release profile of R–GQDs@DOX in PBS solution (pH 5.5 or 7.4).

head.ce:title: Fabrication and characterization of novel cRGD modified graphene quantum dots for chemo-photothermal combination therapy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Xiamen Medical College, Xiamen 361023, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Xiamen Medical College, Xiamen 361023, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Respiratory Diseases, Xiamen Medical College, Xiamen 361023, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Respiratory diseases, Xiamen medical college, Xiamen 361023, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmacy, Xiamen Xianyue Hospital 361012, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmacy, Xiamen Xianyue Hospital 361012, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutics, School of Pharmacy, Harbin University of Commerce, 150076, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutics, School of Pharmacy, Harbin University of Commerce, 150076, P.R. China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Pharmacy, Xiamen Medical College, Xiamen 361023, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, an innovative drug delivery system based on cyclic RGD-modified (as the targeting agent) and doxorubicin-loaded graphene quantum dots (R–GQDs@DOX) was fabricated for chemo-photothermal combination therapy. These tasks include drug loading capacity, photothermal efficiency, cellular uptake and combination therapeutic effect of R–GQDs@DOX. The structure of R–GQDs@DOX was characterized by FT-IR spectrum, UV–vis spectrum and AFM. The results indicated that the doxorubicin (DOX) could be loaded onto graphene quantum dots (GQDs) via van der Waals interaction (π–π stacking) and the drug loading capacity was 96.6 % (wt). The R–GQDs@DOX not only showed obvious pH-responsive release of DOX, but also exhibited great photothermal efficacy for cancer cells. The fluorescence intensity indicated that R–GQDs@DOX was effectively taken up by SK–mel–5 and H460 cells. The combination of chemotherapy and photothermal therapy could more effectively inhibit growth of tumor cells, compared to photothermal therapy or chemotherapy alone.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is one of the biggest threats to human wellbeing and quality of life [1,2]. Traditional treatments include surgery, chemotherapy and radiotherapy. However, surgical treatment can only target patients whose early cancer cells have not metastasized. Chemotherapy lacks selective killing of cancer cells as it also kills normal cells. Radiotherapy can only target undifferentiated tumor cells and has severe side effects. Specifically, killing tumor cells without damaging normal cells is the biggest challenge in current tumor treatment. The emerging nanomaterial technology provides an opportunity to overcome this challenge [3–5]. Nanomaterial’s size, surface, volume and quantum effect can be utilized by cancer diagnosis and treatment, as well as its acoustic, electrical, optical, magnetic and thermal properties. Alongside research of nanobiomaterial, photothermal therapy is starting to get more attention, particularly on how to combine the advantages of chemotherapy and photothermal therapy to cure cancer. Another challenge is how to retain patient compliance during this new combined therapy [6–9].

Chemotherapy is a pharmaceutical treatment that kills tumor cells by slowing or preventing their growth [10–12]. Currently there are various types of chemotherapies in use. The new chemotherapy can reduce the size of tumors before surgery or radiotherapy. Adjuvant chemotherapy can eliminate residual cancer cells after surgery and radiotherapy [13–15]. Another application is to kill metastatic or recurring cancer cells in order to assist radiotherapy and biotherapy. Clinical application of conventional chemotherapy has been affected by its solubility and stability in environment which limited its effectiveness, as well as patient resistance and side effects on healthy cells [16–19].

Photothermal therapy (PTT) destroys cancer cells by a high temperature radiation [20–22]. The key to success of this therapy is the near-infrared absorbance close to cancer cells and high photothermal effect. Compared to conventional therapies, phototherapy has many advantages, including preciseness, controllability, effectiveness and low side effects on normal tissue. The most widely used photothermal materials include carbon, precious metal, semiconductors and composite nanoparticles. Although there have been high hopes for these nanomaterials, work still needs to be done to increase their photothermal effect and optical stability. Recently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted attention and been researched as an excellent photothermal material [23–25]. This new photothermal material is cost effective, good biocompatibility and a high photothermal effect.

Graphene quantum dot is a layer of carbon atoms in monoatomic thickness which is zero dimensional structure that have been recently discovered [26]. It is a basic structure unit of fullerenes, carbon nanotubes and graphite, and valued by physics and material sciences for its mechanical, quantum and electrical properties. The most widely used graphene derivative in biomedicine is functionalization of oxidized graphene [27,28]. Since oxidized graphene contains a large quantity of oxygen-containing reactive groups, it has good biocompatibility and stability in aqueous solution. It is beneficial for chemical modification function and has practical applications in various areas [29]. It can be utilized in covalent reactions with various chemical groups and functional molecules with certain chemical and biological properties, depending on their oxygenated activity and group chemical reactions. One of the common covalent modification methods is to attach biomolecules and chemical groups on graphene via acylation and esterification [15,30]. The surface of graphene may also be functionalized by non-covalent modification such as π-π interactions, ionic bond and hydrogen bond [31–33]. An important application is the delivery of insoluble medications, especially anti-cancer medication. This is achieved by covalent modification with tricyclic antidepressants due to its high physical absorption.

The application of graphene in medication is mainly limited by its band structure and toxicity [34,35]. Graphene quantum dots become the best solution for both of these issues. Generally, GQDs are graphene sheets less than 100 nm thick [36–38]. Due to significant edge effect and quantum confinement, GQDs show various new physical properties including low cytotoxicity, good solubility, photoluminescence, are chemically inert, and have better surface contact properties. These qualities give GQDs a good application perspective in optoelectronic devices, biological sensor and imaging [39–42]. Although graphene has a certain level of cytotoxicity, GQDs demonstrate lower cytotoxicity in biological and in vitro experiments, and this could be further reduced with a size reduction of graphene. GQDs therefore have more application opportunities from a safety perspective. Some recent researches reveal that, due to its light absorption characteristics, GQDs can absorb more near-infrared light and convert it to photothermal radiation to more effectively kill regional malignant cancer cells. GQDs can potentially be used as a new photothermal therapy material [43–45].

It is still not satisfied that photothermal therapy material does not focus on the target lesion. Improvement in target positioning is a new subject for research. Tumour-specific expression of integrins recognizes the extracellular matrix by bioactive peptides and mediates tumor metastasis [46]. Angiogenesis can be used as target of medical carrier. Hence the side effects of anti-tumor drugs can be reduced by active targeting of the tumor site with a medical carrier made of RGD and integrins wrapped by polymer materials and attached with bioactive peptides RGD [47,48]. In conclusion, PTT has a number of advantages compared to conventional treatment. However it still needs to improve its targeting and stability. Both chemotherapy and photothermal therapy have disadvantages. It is both the key and the challenge to synthetically combine advantages and overcome their disadvantages to cure cancer.

It is very important to multi-target inhibition and minimally invasive therapeutic treatments for a variety of cancers. Our preliminary research suggested that multifunctional graphene oxide/chitosan-based nanomaterials with properties of efficient loading, pH-responsive and active targeting was prepared successfully.

In this research, we examined and discussed the biomedical applications of graphene quantum dots with properties of active targeting, and photothermal/chemo synergetic therapy, based on graphene quantum dots.

PEGylated N-succinimidyl 3-(2-pyridyldithio)-propionate (PEG12–SPDP) was purchased from Thermo Scientific; graphene quantum dot was obtained from Nanjing XFNANO Materials Tech Co., Ltd (Nanjing, China); H460 cell lines and MCF-7 (human breast cancer) cell lines were purchased from Shanghai Zhong Qiao Xin Zhou Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). SK–mel–5 cell lines were obtained from Shanghai Chuan Qiu Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China); DOX was obtained from Dalian Meilun Biological Product Factory (Dalian, China) and c[RGDfK (Mpa)] was purchased from ChinaPeptides Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). All other reagents and solvents were of analytical grade.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of multifunctional graphene quantum dots
Synthesis of cRGD–modified graphene quantum dots (R–GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The GQDs were coupled with cRGD using a thiolation reaction. The PEG–SPDP as a crosslinker was used to attach thiolated cRGD peptides (cRGD–SH) to aminated graphene quantum dots (GQDs–NH2). The synthetic procedure is a modification of a previously reported method [49]. Briefly, 1 mL of PEG–SPDP (10 mg/mL) was added to 30 mL (1 mg/mL) GQDs–NH2 solution, adjusting the pH of the reaction system with 0.05 mol/mL phosphoric acid to 7–8. The solution was left to react for 4 h (darkness, 25 °C). The reaction solution then had 1 mL (10 mg/mL) cRGD added and was left to react for 24 h under the same conditions. The reaction solution was then dialyzed for three days with a dialysis tube (MWCO = 3500). The dialysate was freeze-dried for 24 h to get cRGD–modified GQDs and was then characterized by atomic force microscope and infrared spectroscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Loading R–GQDs with DOX (R–GQDs@DOX) and its characterization
ce:section.ce:para: The R–GQDs solution (1 mg/mL) were cultivated with DOX (0.8∼1.4 mg/mL) for 24 h darkness, 25 °C. The solution was cetnrifugated 16,000 rpm for 10 min to remove the free DOX. The supernatant solutions were removed, and the sediment washed with PBS solution (centrifugation, three times). The R–GQDs@DOX precipitate was freeze-dried to get the solids, and characterized by ultraviolet spectrophotometry and fluorospectrophotometry.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physical and chemical properties of R–GQDs@DOX

ce:section.ce:para:
The content of DOX was determined according to our previous report [50]. All the chromatographic analyses were performed using Ultra High Performance Liquid Chromatography (Waters ACQUITY UPLC). The analyses were carried out on the BEH C18 column (2.1 × 50 mm, 1.7 μm). FL detection: 480 nm (Excitation wavelength) and 550 nm (Emission wavelength); Mobile phase: 0.04 % trifluoroacetic acid in water: tetrahydrofuran: acetonitrile (75:0.8: 24.2, v/v/v); Flow rate: 0.4 mL/min. The drug loading efficiency (DLE) of DOX in R–GQDs@DOX was calculated using the formula below:
DLE =
(W added
-W free
)
W R
×100%

DLE: DOX drug loading in R–GQDs@DOX, Wadded: initial quantilty of DOX, Wfree: quantity of DOX in centrifugated supernatant. WR: weight of freeze-dried R–GQDs@DOX. The drug loading efficiency was 96.6 %.

The size distribution and Zeta potential of R–GQDs@DOX was analyzed with a particle analyser (Malvern Zetasizer Nano-ZS90). The morphology of R–GQDs@DOX was tested with AFM (Veeco MultiMode Nanoscope IIIa controller). When using AFM, drop 20 μL of the R–GQDs@DOX solution onto a clean mica slice to observe R–GQDs@DOX. When using a laser particle analyzer, dilute the solution was diluted 20-fold at temperature of 25 ℃.

The drug-release behavior was determined with the dynamic dialysis method. The R–GQDs@DOX was dissolved in 2.5 mL of a PBS solution (pH 7.4 or pH 5.5). It was placed into a dialysis tube (MWCO: 3500), dialyzed in 50 mL of PBS buffer at 37 °C. Samples of 2.5 mL were taken at different times. The blank PBS buffer of 2.5 mL at the same temperature was added. The concentration of DOX released was tested with the same methodology as 2.3.1.

Solutions of R–GQDs and R–GQDs@DOX were prepared at different concentrations for photothermal effect test. The temperature change over time was measured with an infrared camera (Fluke TiX580) and a temperature tester (Fluke 52-II) under near infrared light (808 nm, 2 W/cm2). The temperature was recorded every 1 min for a total of 8 min.

The SK–mel–5 cells and H460 cells were used as model cells for the cytotoxicity of the R–GQDs@DOX. Qualitative analysis was performed using a laser confocal fluorescence microscope (Nikon-Eclipse-Ti). The photothermal effect of R–GQDs@DOX was calculated under NIR irradiation (808 nm).

The CCK-8 method was used to test cytotoxicity of R–GQDs, R–GQDs@DOX and DOX on the SK–mel–5 and H460 cells. Meanwhile, the photothermal effect cytotoxicity of R–GQDs@DOX was tested under the same conditions. The cancer cells were cultivated with DOX, R–GQDs and R–GQDs@DOX at different concentrations for 24 h and irradiated with near-infrared light (808 nm, 2 W/cm2).

SK–mel–5 cell lines and H460 cell lines were plated in medium at a plating density of 5 × 103 cells. The plates were placed in an incubator with 5 % CO2 at 37 ℃. After 24 h, R–GQDs@DOX of 100 μg/mL concentration was added and then mixed with 100 μL of Hoechst 33342 1 μg/mL for 20 min. Fluorescence imaging of cells with GQDs and DOX were studied as control experiments. MCF-7 cells αvβ3-negative were used to estimate the target abilities of R-GQDs-DOX, compared to cells which over-expressed receptors of RGD [51,52]. The imaging test was performed on a laser confocal fluorescence microscope. The cells were then determined by a High Content Cellomics Analysis Reader (Thermo Fisher Scientific Cellomics).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterization of the R–GQDs@DOX
ce:section.ce:para: The amino of GQDs was activated by the PEG–SPDP. Then, the cRGD was linked to the activated GQDs by thiolation reaction. The design strategy of R–GQDs@DOX was illustrated in Fig. 1 . In the Fig. 2 A, GQDs exhibits characteristic absorption peaks of NH at 3281 and 1564 cm−1. The peaks at 1397 cm-1 was assigned to the bending vibration of CN bond of amide group, which are identical with the results of previous report [53]. GQDs–PEG shows CO and CN characteristic absorption peaks at 1664 and 1222cm-1, respectively, demonstrating that the PEG–SPDP successfully reacted with the GQDs. After covalent bonding of cRGD peptides to the GQDs–PEG, the typical peaks of GQDs–PEG remained, and the two new peaks at 1453 and 1092 cm-1could correspond with NH and CN stretching vibration, respectively. These changes confirm that the cRGD peptides conjugated with the GQDs [33]. Loading of the DOX onto R–GQDs was successfully confirmed by the obtained FT-IR spectra, as shown in Fig. S1.

As shown in Fig. 2B, the UV–vis spectrum shows free DOX characteristic absorption peaks at 485 nm. This is due to the existence of dihydroxyindole chromophore. There is also evidence of absorption at 232, 253 and 290 nm. The R–GQDs had the absorption peak at 326 nm due to n-π* transition, which was similar to the reported GQDs [26]. The R–GQDs@DOX shows UV absorption peaks at 498 nm. Compared to free DOX, R–GQDs@DOX shows a 13 nm bathochromic shift. This is commonly recognized as due to the π-π overlay superposition of DOX and R–GQDs hydrophobic interaction, and represents DOX successfully loaded on R–GQDs.

The fluorescence spectroscopy results show an emission peak of free DOX and R–GQDs@DOX under 485 nm, as shown in the Fig. 2C. However, the fluorescence of R–GQDs@DOX is considerably less intense than free DOX. This is probably due to DOX is loaded on surface of R–GQDs which triggers electron transfer and eventually fluorescence quenching. These results further justify strong π-π multiplying effect between DOX and R–GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physical and chemical properties of R–GQDs@DOX
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 shows AFM of R–GQDs@DOX and the thickness is 2.89 nm. This is thicker than GQDs@DOX by 0.5 nm due to the crosslinker (PEG–SPDP) and peptides (cRGD). This is more evidence of cRGD being modified to GQDs in order to get R–GQDs. TEM of R–GQDs@DOX determined by transmission electron microscopy (Hitachi HT7700 Exalens) was shown in Fig.S3.

Fig. 4 shows the zeta potential (–43.1 mV) of GQDs@DOX and R–GQDs@DOX. This illustrates that GQDs@DOX contains a large quantiy of an oxygen-containing functional group including carboxyl and hydroxyl. According to Fig. 4, the zeta potential of R–GQDs@DOX is –30.1 mV after being modified with cRGD. This is due to the modification of cRGD with GQDs@DOX to form amide bond which increased the electric potential. Although GQDs were aminated, there were many negatively charged carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on GQDs [54,55]. The significant change of zeta potential demonstrates the evidence of GQDs@DOX being modified with cRGD. The particle size of GQDs@DOX was 29.7 nm, which is larger than its size in AFM, as shown in Fig. 4. This is due to ligands at particle surface featuring fluid dynamics volume. After being modified with cRGD, the particle size of R–GQDs@DOX increased to 33.01 nm. This demonstrates R–GQDs@DOX was evenly distributed in the solution and not aggregated.

R–GQDs could transfer NIR energy into heat. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the infrared thermal curves of the R–GQDs verified great temperature change by laser irradiation within eight minutes. Fig. 5B is the temperature chart of R–GQDs under 808 nm with different concentrations. The temperature of the R–GQDs (100 μg/mL for concentration) rose (from 37 to 58.1 °C) within eight minutes after irradiation. The photothermal effect results showed that there were no notable differences in the temperature chart of R–GQDs@DOX (100 μg/mL for R–GQDs) and R–GQDs, as shown in Supporting Information Fig.S2. This proves the possibility of a photothermal therapy effect of R–GQDs@DOX.

In Fig. 8D, the R–GQDs@DOX composite shows obvious pH–sensitivity when releasing DOX. It is shown that the release efficiency of DOX in a pH 7.4 PBS buffer is significantly lower than in pH 5.5, which is reported in literature for similar system [31]. In a pH 7.4 buffer, less than 19.3 % DOX was released within 48 h. However, the release increased markedly with 48.2 % DOX within the same timeframe in a pH 5.5 buffer.

The DOX could be monitored without using external fluorescent dyes. The released DOX could be observed by CLSM due to the red fluorescence emitted from the R–GQDs@DOX. As shown in Fig. 6 and Fig. 7
, the strong red fluorescence reveals that R–GQDs@DOX is effectively taken up by SK–mel–5 and H460 cells. By contrast, GQDs@DOX in SK–mel–5 and H460 cells was not clear visualized. Cyclic RGD peptide-based drug delivery systems had also been used for cancer cell targeting because of their high selectivity and affinity of integrin αvβ3 [56].The H460 and SK–mel cell lines were high expression of integrin αvβ3 [57,58]. The red fluorescence could be clear visualized due to a good bind affinity of R–GQDs@DOX to cell surface integrin αvβ3. To further confirm the effect of RGD, the R–GQDs@DOX was added to MCF-7 cells (αvβ3-negative) as a negative control experiment. As shown in Fig. S4, almost no red fluorescence was observed for MCF-7 cells, which support the specific interaction of R–GQDs@DOX with the αvβ3-positive cells. The mean fluorescence intensity of polymers was examined by the high-content screening (HCS). In Fig. 8 C, the mean fluorescence intensity of R–GQDs@DOX in SK–mel–5 and H460 cells is much higher than GQDs@DOX. Therefore, the R–GQDs@DOX shows great potential for cellular imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro chemotherapy and photothermal therapy
ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots have good biocompatibility and low biotoxicity, therefore can be used as a carrier of chemical drugs. In addition, GQDs has a photothermal effect as well as light absorbance at the near infrared region. Releasing energy at the tumor site will cause a rise in temperature and eventually kill the cells. The SK–mel–5 cells and H460 cells were incubated with DOX, R–GQDs and R–GQDs@DOX. A comparison was done with and without NIR irradiation (808 nm, 2 W/cm2). As shown in Fig. 8 (A–B), R–GQDs with a concentration between 0 μg/mL and 200 μg/mL without near infrared light had no toxicity to SK–mel–5 and H460 cells. In the R–GQDs@DOX and DOX groups, cell viability rate decreased when the solution concentration increased. The IC50 of R–GQDs@DOX was 71.59 μg/mL and 92.29 μg/mL for SK–mel–5 and H460 cells, respectively. The toxicity of R–GQDs@DOX was significantly increased after NIR irradiation, which triggered radiant heat. The IC50 decreased to 39.63 μg/mL and 53.75 μg/mL, which is significantly lower than separate chemotherapy (R–GQDs@DOX, 71.59 μg/mL and 92.29 μg/mL) and photothermal therapy (R–GQDs + NIR, 113.65 μg/mL and 120.13 μg/mL). These results prove that composite R–GQDs@DOX can effectively combine chemo-photothermal therapy for a synergistic effect.

In our study, a GQDs-based multifunctional system (R−GQDs@DOX) was designed for chemo-photothermal combination therapy, which is comprised of cRGD as the targeting agent and DOX as the chemotherapy agent. The results demonstrated that the R−GQDs@DOX could be efficiently loaded with DOX. The R–GQDs@DOX exhibited great photothermal efficacy for cancer cells. The chemo-photothermal combination therapy could more effectively inhibit growth of tumor cells. Overall, the fabricated R−GQDs@DOX exhibited the following characteristics: (i) pH-responsive drug-release behavior; (ii) good ability of cellular uptake; (iii) great photothermal efficacy in vitro; and (iv) enhanced the effect of chemo-photothermal combination therapy for cancer cells. The novel developed platform will have a great potential for improving the treatment of cancer.
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the General Program of Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province of China (Grant No.: 2019D016), the Training Program for Middle-aged and Young Key Talents of Fujian Provincial Health Commission (Grant No.: 2018-ZQN-94), the New Century Excellent Talent Support Program of Higher Education Institutions of Fujian Province (Grant No.: MINJIAOKE[2018]47HAO), the Program of Institute of Respiratory diseases of Xiamen medical college (Grant No.: HXJB-04), the Educational Research Program for Middle-aged and Young Teachers of the Educational Committee of Fujian Province (Grant No.: JAT170698), the Major Scientific Research Project of Xiamen Medical College (Grant No.: XWJW201403-02ZD201403-02), the Doctoral Scientific Research Foundation of Xiamen Medical College (Grant No.: Z2013-15) and the National level Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program for College Students of Fujian Province (Grant No.: 201712631004).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2020.127732.

ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

Cancer Res.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Chen Wang received his pH.D. from Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, China, in 2015. He worked as a teacher in Xiamen Medical College in July 2008. His research has been focused on nanoparticles/drug delivery system to improve active targeting. He has received the honor of Xiamen's innovative talents in 2019 and the New Century Excellent Talent Support Program of Higher Education Institutions of Fujian Province in 2018. Projects ongoing: 1. The General Program of Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province of China (2019D016). 2. The Training Program for Middle-aged and Young Key Talents of Fujian Provincial Health Commission (2018-ZQN-94). 3. The New Century Excellent Talent Support Program of Higher Education Institutions of Fujian Province ([2018]47). 4. The Program of Institute of Respiratory diseases of Xiamen medical college (HXJB-04). 5. The Educational Research Program for Middle-aged and Young Teachers of the Educational Committee of Fujian Province (JAT170698).6. The Major Scientific Research Project of Xiamen Medical College (XWJW201403-02\ZD201403-02).7. The National level Entrepreneurship Training Program for College Students of Fujian Province 201712631004.
